data.title: The mother of all mega-dams is China's hidden weapon in the Himalayas
data.description:
New disclosures reveal that China’s planned super-dam on the Yarlung Tsangpo — the world’s highest-altitude major river, better known as the Brahmaputra — is not a single structure. Rather, it is a a vast, tunnel-linked hydropower and water-diversion complex, spanning roughly 150 kilometers through the Himalayas.

The project would transform an internationally shared river, originating in water-rich Tibet, into a strategic instrument of state power.

For years, China’s super-dam was discussed as a single, if colossal, piece of infrastructure. That description is now obsolete. What Beijing is constructing on the so-called “Everest of Rivers,” near Tibet’s border with India, is not just the largest dam ever attempted. It is an extensive subterranean network designed to give China effective command over the river before it reaches any downstream country.

A cascade of barrages, reservoirs and power stations — linked by giant tunnels bored through the world’s highest mountain range — will together form a titanic, integrated hydropower system.

It remains unclear whether some of the impounded water will be diverted into what Beijing calls the Great South-North Water Diversion Project. While the eastern and central routes — already operational — transfer water from China’s southern rivers to its arid north, including Beijing, the controversial western line aims to reroute waters from Tibet-originating international rivers that sustain hundreds of millions of people across South and Southeast Asia.

The new project is centered near the Great Bend of the Brahmaputra, where the river makes a dramatic U-turn around a Himalayan peak before plunging toward India. Over a stretch of just 30 miles, the river drops some 6,500 feet — one of the steepest descents of any major river on Earth.

China plans to exploit this extraordinary gradient not with a single concrete wall, but by diverting the river through multiple tunnels, some extending more than 12 miles, to feed a cascade of five power stations. Their combined generating capacity is estimated at over 60 gigawatts — almost three times that of the Three Gorges Dam, currently the world’s largest.

This design fundamentally alters the risk calculus for downstream states. Beijing has described the project as “run-of-the-river,” a term meant to reassure neighboring countries that water storage — and therefore Chinese control — will be limited. But the scale of tunneling and the interlinked reservoirs undermines that claim. Even without massive surface reservoirs, the ability to divert, withhold or abruptly release enormous volumes of water through subterranean channels gives China de facto control over the river’s flow just before it leaves Tibet, which Beijing annexed in 1951.

By occupying Tibet, China effectively redrew the water map of much of Asia. The Tibetan Plateau is the source of ten major river systems that together sustain nearly one-fifth of the world’s population. China’s intensive dam-building and resource extraction across the plateau since the 1990s thus poses a growing risk to Asian water security, ecological stability and regional peace. Control over the headwaters of key transboundary rivers confers immense leverage, turning water into a potential geopolitical tool and raising the specter of environmental disaster and conflict.

Nothing illustrates these dangers more starkly than the current gargantuan project, which officially began in 2025, although evidence suggests preparatory work started much earlier. What is taking shape is, in strategic terms, a hydrological weapon system. It is capable of degrading fragile ecosystems while giving China unprecedented coercive leverage over South Asia — especially India.

There is also a profound seismic gamble. The project lies in one of the world’s most active earthquake zones, where the Indian and Eurasian tectonic plates collide.

Geologists warn of reservoir-triggered seismicity, in which the immense weight of stored water increases stress along fault lines, even if the statistical probability of a major quake remains uncertain. A partial failure, caused by an earthquake or a massive landslide into a reservoir, could unleash a cascading flood racing toward India’s densely populated Assam Valley, with catastrophic consequences for downstream communities, cities and infrastructure.

What makes this project uniquely destabilizing is not only its scale but its opacity. China has released no meaningful technical details, including information on design parameters or water-storage capacity. The project has proceeded without environmental impact assessments open to international scrutiny and without consultation with downstream states that stand to bear the greatest risks.

This is a textbook case of hydrological hegemony: unilateral control over a shared lifeline exercised by an upstream power.

The international response has been strikingly muted. Governments that rightly scrutinize Chinese ports, telecommunications networks and supply chains have largely treated this mega-project as a regional matter rather than a global concern. That is a mistake. Water insecurity is a well-documented accelerator of conflict, forced migration and state fragility. A project capable of degrading ecosystems and affecting tens of millions of people downstream implicates not only Asian stability but the international order itself.

China’s Brahmaputra mega-system should therefore be understood for what it is: an unprecedented experiment in high-altitude engineering, ecological transformation and geopolitical leverage rolled into one. It is a strategic instrument whose consequences will flow far beyond the Himalayas.

Brahma Chellaney is the author of nine books, including the award-winning “Water: Asia’s New Battleground.”

data.title: Planned Parenthood Action Fund rolls out first Senate endorsements ahead of November midterms
data.description:
Planned Parenthood Action Fund rolled out their first set of Senate endorsements ahead of the November midterms on Thursday, backing Sen. Jon Ossoff (D-Ga.) in addition to Democratic Senate candidates running in North Carolina, New Hampshire and Ohio.

The abortion rights group is endorsing former North Carolina Gov. Roy Cooper (D), Rep. Chris Pappas (D-N.H.) in New Hampshire and former Sen. Sherrod Brown (D-Ohio) in Ohio, news of which was first shared with The Hill.

The announcement coincides with the 53rd Anniversary of the landmark Supreme Court decision Roe v. Wade, which offered federal protections for abortion. That decision was later overturned by the high court in 2022.

“Fifty-three years after Roe v. Wade was decided, the freedom to control our own bodies has been ripped away from us. Anti-abortion rights lawmakers in the Senate are forcing their unpopular agenda while their constituents face rising health care costs, soaring insurance premiums, and more barriers to essential care,” Alexis McGill, president and CEO of Planned Parenthood Action Fund, said in a statement.

“We can count on these champions to deliver what voters need: Legislators who will always fight for our freedom to make our own decisions about our lives, bodies, and futures,” she added.

The Senate races in all four states will be highly competitive and will determine whether Democrats can make inroads toward narrowing the Senate GOP majority or if Republicans can potentially expand their map.

The nonpartisan election handicapper Cook Political Report rates the Senate seats in Georgia and North Carolina as “toss ups,” while rating the Ohio Senate race as “lean Republican” and the New Hampshire Senate race as “lean Democratic.”

data.title: Live updates: Major US allies absent as Trump sets Board of Peace in motion
data.description:
President Trump is in Davos, Switzerland, where he is meeting with world leaders and unveiled his new Board of Peace in Gaza.

Earlier Thursday, the president participated in a signing ceremony for his peace board initiative alongside a handful of world leaders. No major European allies attended the Thursday signing ceremony, however, as tensions flare with NATO amid Trump’s bid to acquire Greenland

After he delivered remarks Wednesday at the World Economic Forum, Trump announced that allies reached a “framework” of a deal on his quest to acquire Greenland. The president also said he would no longer impose tariffs on Denmark and other NATO countries, walking back threats to forcefully take over the island nation.

Trump is slated to meet with Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky Thursday morning amid his efforts to bring about an end to Russia’s nearly four-year-war in Ukraine.

Vice President Vance, meanwhile, will travel to Toledo, Ohio, and then Minneapolis, where protests have flared over federal immigration enforcement efforts across the state.

In Washington, the House will host former special counsel Jack Smith at 10 a.m., when he will testify publicly on his investigations into Trump.

Follow live below.

data.title: Trump confident in Secret Service for safety as president despite threats
data.description:
President Trump said he has confidence in the Secret Service’s protection regarding his safety after surviving two assassination attempts before his return to the White House last year.

The president was asked by NewsNation’s Katie Pavlich on “Katie Pavlich Tonight” if he is ever afraid about his safety given threats that loom, to which he replied, “I don’t worry about it.”

“And we have great people, and I feel, I feel that we’re in good shape,” Trump continued. “They’re in good shape. I think the Secret Service has done a great job. The military has done a great job. And one thing I know, if something happens, the person that did it is going to be gone, because we are surrounded. You know, even as we speak, we are surrounded.”

“But no, I feel very good,” the president added. “It’s a job that has its dangers. If you think, right, you look at the percentages, it’s probably the most dangerous job in the world. I was telling people, I guess it’s 5 and a half percent. It’s a bad number, 5 and a half percent, that’s all.”

In the same interview, Trump said he Iran would be “blown up” if they carried out threats of assassinating him. A previous alleged plot was thwarted in 2024.

Trump survived two assassination attempts during his third run for the presidency in 2024. During a campaign rally in Butler, Pa., in July 2024, a bullet grazed the president’s ear before he dropped to the floor surrounded by Secret Service agents. As the agents walked off with the president, Trump pumped his fist to the crowd.

The would-be assassin, Thomas Matthew Crooks, was on a rooftop near the rally site with an AR-style rifle. Crooks was shot and killed shortly after the shooting began.

One rally-goer, Corey Comperatore, a former fire chief from the area, was killed.

The Secret Service faced intense scrutiny over the assassination attempt. An internal review found that the agency had “multiple operational and communications failures” leading up to the rally. Former Secret Service acting Director Ronald Rowe called for a “paradigm shift” in how the agency carries out its protective security responsibilities.

Trump praised the Secret Service following the shooting, thanking them on Truth Social for an “incredible job” on what “was certainly an interesting day!”

Last November, the FBI concluded its investigation into Crooks and determined that Crooks acted alone and with no motive.

In September 2024, the Secret Service prevented a second assassination attempt after a man a gun was allegedly stalking Trump. The man, Ryan Routh, was found guilty of five charges,  including attempted assassination of a major presidential candidate, last year. The Department of Justice is seeking a life sentence for Routh.

data.title: Pro-lifers must not lose momentum in the new year
data.description:
On the heels of some big wins in 2025 for the pro-life movement, in early December, Bloomberg broke the news that the U.S. Food and Drug Administration is allegedly delaying a long-awaited review of safety data related to the chemical abortion drug, mifepristone, until after the midterm elections.  

This announcement should be a rallying cry for the pro-life movement. We must reinstate commonsense protections against dangerous chemical abortion drugs, ensure states protect women from the risks of enshrining unregulated and later abortions in their constitutions, and push back against any federal funding of induced abortion, including that intended to rescue Planned Parenthood from collapse. 

With several 2025 wins under our belts, we have the blueprint for success.  

In January, President Trump reinstated the Mexico City Policy to prevent American taxpayer dollars from funding abortion overseas. Just a month later, the New York Times published an explosive piece on Planned Parenthood, revealing allegations of botched procedures, outdated equipment, under-qualified staff, and, in one instance, literal sewage leaking into a clinic waiting room.

This shocking expose was the final incentive legislators needed to successfully defund Planned Parenthood at the federal level for the year. Today, 42 Planned Parenthoods have already closed as a consequence, and Planned Parenthood predicts that up to 200 more clinics could be shuttered soon across America.

Over the summer, the U.S. Supreme Court provided another victory against the predatory abortion industry in Medina v. Planned Parenthood South Atlantic — a case for which I was proud to file an amicus brief with other pro-life OBGYNs. The court ruled that states can decline to fund the organization and instead direct their resources to life-affirming healthcare for women and children.

Americans know that women deserve better than the shoddy care and direct harm they receive at Planned Parenthood. According to recent data, most women who have had an abortion would have preferred to parent if they had more resources or support.

Fortunately, pregnancy help centers — many of them staffed by life-affirming medical professionals — are leading the way in meeting these needs. In 2024 alone, these centers provided more than a million women and children with $452 million in total material goods and services and boasted a 98 percent satisfaction rate from clients — quite a contrast to Planned Parenthood.  

In 2026, we must continue championing the fight to protect American women and preborn children from dangerous chemical abortion drugs.

Last year 30,000 medical professionals called on the FDA to reinstate common-sense safeguards on the drugs. This included revoking the insane Biden-era policy allowing the drugs to be shipped via mail and reinstate the requirement that women have an in-person medical consultation before acquiring them.

The data recently showed that the chemical abortion drug, mifepristone, has 22 times the risks of serious complications that the FDA currently reports on the drug label. Moreover, revoking the in-person dispensing requirement leaves many women to experience potential serious adverse reactions, scared and alone. It also denies them the option of follow-up care from a medical professional who is qualified and knows their history.

Worst of all, it leaves women at the mercy of abusers who are known to acquire these drugs and poison their victims, forcing an unwanted abortions upon them. This has happened many times since mail-order abortion was legalized — including last summer in Texas.

The American people recognize the need to protect women from these dangers. Just recently, fresh polling revealed that most Americans support basic safeguards when it comes to abortion drugs. The FDA must fulfill its promise to review abortion drug data and do its job to protect patients.

Likewise, 90 percent of Americans oppose allowing abortion later in pregnancy, yet another state is considering doing just that in the New Year. Following the lead of several other states, Virginia is expected to have an abortion amendment on its ballot this year, allowing abortions through all trimesters of pregnancy for any reason. This highlights the need for us to keep educating on the harms of abortion for both women and preborn children and the fact that induced abortion — the intentional ending of a human life — is not healthcare.   

Finally, in 2026, we must oppose attempts to rescue Planned Parenthood from federal funding cuts. Congress’s decision last year was the right one, and we will continue supporting efforts to ensure that no American tax dollars are devoted to any organization that harms hundreds of thousands of babies and moms with substandard care annually.  

In the coming year, pro-life voices must lead the way in highlighting and advocating for life-affirming healthcare that recognizes the value and worth of every one of our patients and restores the true purpose of medicine — health and healing.  

Women deserve better healthcare, better protections, and better futures. Now is the time to be bold, take advantage of the moment, and fight to give it to them. 2025 saw some significant victories for the pro-life movement and our patients. We must work to ensure that 2026 sees even more.  

Dr. Christina Francis is a board-certified obstetrician-gynecologist practicing as an obstetric hospitalist. She also serves as CEO of the American Association of Pro-Life Obstetricians and Gynecologists.

data.title: On Greenland, Trump eats some humble pie but learns a valuable lesson
data.description:
While en route to Davos, Switzerland Tuesday night, Air Force One was forced to make a U-turn and return to Joint Base Andrews due to an electrical failure. 

Turns out it was foreshadowing the U-turn that Trump made on Wednesday regarding Greenland while addressing the World Economic Forum. 

Trump finally admitted, despite days of hinting otherwise, that the U.S. was not going to militarily invade and seize the Danish territory.

Trump’s bluster toward Greenland leading up to his speech was likely designed to create negotiating leverage. He was trying, albeit inartfully, to set the stage for Davos, where he would say, “Now that I have your attention, here is what I really want out of Europe on Greenland.”

Alas, he overplayed his hand. Europe did not bite. Instead, London, Paris and Brussels began biting back.

Despite his concession not to invade, the European Union announced that it was suspending work on approving and implementing the multi-trillion dollar U.S.-EU trade deal struck last August with the Trump administration.

Midterms are nearing, and Trump is pushing an affordability agenda. To that end, he had just signed an executive order limiting corporate ownership of single-family housing––and requested that Congress cap credit card interest rates for one year at 10 percent.

Notably, as well, 90 percent of U.S. voters are opposed to the U.S. taking military action to seize the sovereign territory of a NATO ally. Midterms are fast-approaching and military adventurism is not high on the list of most primary voters.

Given all of the above, Team Trump was essentially forced to eat some humble pie during his speech in Davos. By backing down from the military threats, he acknowledged that his brinksmanship was never going to work. 

Instead – –wisely, in our opinion –– Trump decided to come in from the cold. Instead of ditching NATO, as many feared he would, he embraced it.

Of course, he did it his own combative way. Much of his speech was geared toward complaining that NATO effectively owes the U.S. Greenland as payment for decades of U.S. stewardship and protection of Europe during the Cold War.

Underpinning that argument is Trump’s strong belief that “The United States is treated very unfairly by NATO,” as he put it. “We give so much,” he said, “and we get so little in return.”

He dismissed that his primary motivation was rare earth minerals — denying, in fact, rare earths are even rare. Rather, he said, only their processing is rare. In that regard, he is correct. The real issue is that China controls more than 90 percent of rare earth processing. But then, Beijing also controls 70 percent of known reserves. Denying Greenland to Chinese President Xi Jinping is therefore a smart, strategic national security strategy.

Humble pie or not, Trump appears to have obtained what he set out for. Or, at the very minimum, he is declaring what he got as a victory.

Soon after his speech ended, Trump announced that, “Based upon a very productive meeting that I have had with Secretary General of NATO, Mark Rutte, we have formed the framework of a future deal with respect to Greenland and, in fact, the entire Arctic Region.”

In making the announcement, Trump added that he was suspending new trade tariffs on Denmark, Finland, France, Germany, the Netherlands, Norway, Sweden and the United Kingdom that were set to be imposed next month.

In subsequent comments, Trump remarked, “It’s a great deal for everybody. Gives us everything we needed.” Central to that is a strategic staging ground for Trump’s proposed “Golden Dome” missile defense system. The New York Times reports that the U.S. is merely seeking sovereignty over its military bases in Greenland, which would only involve “small pockets of land.”

Settling the Greenland issue would be a big win, given the need for a reimagined multilayered air and space defensive shield to protect North America against new and emerging global threats — including drones, hypersonic missiles and the increasing ranges of North Korean nuclear-capable missiles.

The deal is envisioned as a permanent fix. Trump jubilantly called it “the ultimate long-term deal. It’s an infinite deal. It is a deal forever.” Oh, well, there goes Greenland as the 51st state.

But we believe there is another reason Trump decided to come in from the cold: It is likely to get very hot — as in militarily hot — in Iran.

Trump’s initial inclination to intervene in the Iranian protests that erupted on Dec. 28 was severely limited by lack of U.S. military assets in the region. The Pentagon could have struck, but not decisively enough to topple Iranian Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei. Too many resources had been committed to the Venezuela operation that removed and arrested Nicolas Maduro.  

But the events in Iran also reminded Trump that projecting U.S. military force into the Middle East –– especially the Persian Gulf –– and elsewhere depends upon the more than 100 U.S. military facilities abroad that span 51 countries.

Sixty of those bases are in Europe. Projecting U.S. military power requires logistics, and NATO member-states host most of it.

Welcome back from the cold, Mr. President. We are far stronger working with our NATO allies than we are threatening to invade them.

Mark Toth writes on national security and foreign policy. Col. (Ret.) Jonathan Sweet served 30 years as a military intelligence officer and led the U.S. European Command Intelligence Engagement Division from 2012 to 2014.

data.title: Powell favored by voters over Noem, Hegseth and other officials: Poll
data.description:
Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell has higher favorability than a slate of top Trump administration officials, including Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth and Homeland Security Kristi Noem, new polling shows.

An Emerson College Polling national survey finds Powell with a positive net favorability of 18 points, as 45 percent of likely voters view him favorably and 27 percent feel the opposite. 

Secretary of State Marco Rubio is narrowly above water, with 43 percent rating him favorably and 41 percent unfavorably. 

Hegseth and Noem have the lowest net scores of the seven top officials tested, at negative-7 points and negative-6 points underwater, respectively.

Vice President JD Vance pulled in 42 percent of voters who viewed him favorably, slightly behind 46 percent who had an unfavorable perception.

Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent had the lowest favorable rating, at 36 percent, but just 35 percent viewed him unfavorably and 29 percent were unsure.

The scores come amid President Trump’s battle with Powell and the Justice Department’s criminal probe into the Federal Reserve.

“I have deep respect for the rule of law and for accountability in our democracy. No one—certainly not the chair of the Federal Reserve—is above the law,” Powell said in a statement earlier this month, after the DOJ served the Fed with grand jury subpoenas and threatened to indict the central bank chief over testimony he gave last year. 

“But this unprecedented action should be seen in the broader context of the administration’s threats and ongoing pressure.”

Meanwhile, Noem is at the fore of the Trump administration’s controversial crackdown on immigration.

Noem over the weekend touted thousands of arrests in Minnesota, where an Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) officer fatally shot Minnesota woman Renee Good, sparking a renewed surge of protests across the country. 

Last week, Democratic Rep. Robin Kelly (D-Ill.) introduced articles of impeachment against Noem, citing her immigration handling, in a sign of heightened frustration with the secretary.

Trump’s job approval is still underwater by roughly 8 points, but his score has ticked up slightly in this month’s survey, from 41 percent in December to 43 percent this month. 

The Emerson College Polling national survey was conducted Jan. 17-19 among 1,000 U.S. likely voters and has a credibility interval, similar to a poll’s margin of error, of plus or minus 3 percentage points.

data.title: Trump walks back Greenland threats, but Congress fears long-term NATO damage
data.description:
President Trump on Wednesday de-escalated his threats to seize Greenland and hammer Europe with new tariffs, but some lawmakers in both parties fear the damage to NATO and the trans-Atlantic alliance has already been done.

“It’s hard to un-ring the bell,” said Rep. Adam Smith (Wash.), the senior Democrat on the House Armed Services Committee. “He was willing to threaten and use coercive power to try to take the sovereignty of another nation — a NATO ally. It has damaged the relationship, and now our partners and allies think that they’d better have a back-up plan, because they can’t depend upon us.”

Trump has long sought to take over Greenland, a semiautonomous territory of Denmark, and in recent weeks has floated the use of military force to do so.

But on Wednesday, he reversed course in his address to European leaders at the Davos Economic Forum, where he suggested the U.S. would not seize the territory by force and instead called for immediate negotiations. 

A short time later, Trump announced that he had reached a “framework of a future deal” with respect to Greenland after a meeting with NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte, and that he would not impose tariffs he had previously threatened on European allies because they had demanded that the U.S. respect Denmark’s sovereignty over Greenland.

The rapid, back-to-back developments drew a sigh of relief from some lawmakers in the Capitol, who had warned that the attacks on NATO allies — whether through military force or economic sanctions — would obliterate the decades-old alliance. But others said even the threat of those actions has poisoned the long-standing relationship between Washington and European democracies.

Rep. Don Bacon (R-Neb.) said that even though Trump has backed down on his military threat, Greenland is still “their own” territory.

“The whole thing’s crazy. They would give us permission to build a new base. So this whole thing’s done out of vanity or something, I don’t know, but he’s hurting America. He’s hurting our relationships, which hurts our national security,” Bacon said.

Rep. Michael McCaul (R-Texas), the former chair of the House Foreign Affairs Committee, said that “talking about militarily invading a NATO ally obviously is not constructive with our relationships with our European NATO allies.”

He added, “If we broke from a — not only military but economic partnership, that’s a grave concern. It sounds to me, he’s backed off now with a military invasion, which I advocated against last Sunday on ABC. And now we’re into the purchasing aspect, if there’s a willing seller, which there’s not right now. But in terms of the relationship, I would far prefer to see our energies being directed at our adversaries rather than blowing up our NATO alliances that are very important and… it’s prevented us from being (in) another world war, and it brought down the Soviet Union.”

The economic and political fallout of Trump’s threats to Greenland and Europe has already surfaced. Last week, Canada signed a new trade agreement with China. And on Monday in Davos, Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney urged European leaders to follow suit by seeking alternatives to trade deals with the U.S.

“Let me be direct,” Carney warned. “We are in the midst of a rupture, not a transition…A country that can’t feed itself, fuel itself or defend itself, has few options. When the rules no longer protect you, you must protect yourself.”

Rep. Don Beyer (D-Va.), who had served as U.S. ambassador to Switzerland and Liechtenstein in the Obama administration, hailed Carney’s warnings as the highlight of Davos. 

 “Our allies are getting to the end of the naivete about caving to Donald Trump, trying to ameliorate him. … And you’re going to see a lot more pushback against the bully,” Beyer said. “It’s a low point in terms of our relationship with Europe. We’ve kept the world safe for 80 years. In the meantime, we’ve opened the door wide for China and Canada, for China and the EU, for China and sub-Saharan Africa. 

”It’s hurting America.”

The long-term effects of Trump’s aggressive negotiating tactics remain unclear. Beyer predicted that the next president after Trump — whether a Democrat or a Republican — would almost certainly mend the bruised relations Trump has caused. And in the meantime, the ties between the other NATO countries will likely become even stronger. 

“I think it’s a threat to American participation in NATO in the short-term,” he said. “[But] the next president — Democratic or Republican, but especially a Democrat — would do everything we could to strengthen and renew America’s commitment to NATO.”

Rep. Brian Fitzpatrick (R-Pa.) downplayed even the near-term threat, saying there’s a considerable number of lawmakers in both parties ready to go to bat for NATO, even if it defies Trump. He’s a part of the NATO Parliamentary Assembly, and said he’ll be travelling overseas in the coming weeks to deliver that message directly to America’s partners. 

“We’re going to reaffirm to our colleagues there that it’s going to remain strong and that we’re glad they’re spending more on defense” he said. “We have a determined bloc here in this chamber to make sure that that alliance is preserved and … strengthened.”

But Fitzpatrick also pushed back against Trump’s negotiating tactics, suggesting they aren’t helpful in advancing American security or economic interests. 

“I don’t support threatening our allies. I don’t support that kind of performative negotiation,” he said. “You all have been around Trump long enough to know how he conducts himself and how he operates. And this is probably his art of the deal, I guess,” he continued. “It’s not how I conduct myself.”

data.title: Democrats hold 6-point lead over GOP on generic ballot: Poll
data.description:
Democrats are ahead of Republicans by 6 percentage points on a generic congressional ballot, according to a new poll.

In the poll from Emerson College, 48.1 percent of respondents backed a Democratic candidate in a hypothetical 2026 matchup. The Republican candidate garnered 41.7 percent backing in the hypothetical matchup and 10.2 percent were undecided.

The poll comes in the wake of rocky political territory for Republicans, who have been facing criticism over affordability, files linked to convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein and President Trump’s recent foreign policy moves.

Democrats also notched victories in key political races last year, locking down important races including the gubernatorial contests for New Jersey and Virginia. 

In a polling average from Decision Desk HQ, Trump has a 41.4 percent approval rating and a disapproval rating of 56.2 percent.

Democratic strategist James Carville on Saturday predicted that this year’s midterms are going to be a “wipeout” for the GOP and that Democrats will win at least 25 seats.

“Frankly, it’s going to be a wipeout,” Carville said on Fox News’s “Saturday in America.” “Your viewers need to know that the Democrats are going to pick up at a minimum 25 seats, maybe as high as 45. In all likelihood, the Democrats will carry the Senate.”

When asked about how they saw the direction of the country, 43.6 percent in the poll said it was overall going “in the right direction,” while 56.4 percent said it was overall going toward the “wrong track.”

The Emerson poll took place from Jan. 17 to 19, featuring 1,000 likely voters and plus or minus 3 percentage points as its credibility interval.

data.title: As Minneapolis grapples with ICE, schools try to balance learning with student safety
data.description:
Schools in Minneapolis have set up remote learning and increased their transportation options, facing falling attendance and ongoing protests as the community grapples with the fallout from the death of Renee Good. 

The city is shrouded in chaos after a U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) officer shot and killed Good on January 7, becoming the epicenter of pushback against the Trump administration’s immigration actions.

Now, schools there are working to balance student learning with parental fears about dropping their children off.  

The fear escalated after a local school saw ICE agents come onto its property when students were let out for the day, with at least one teacher arrested during the incident.   

The Minneapolis school system is offering remote learning until at least February 12. The Hill has reached out to the district for further comment.

At the Twin Cities-area Fridley Public Schools, where 80 percent of the student population is children of color, 10 percent signed up for virtual learning and huge attendance drops have been recorded. 

“Our staff are terrified to come to work, so now we’ve stood up transportation for them to and from work. We have families who normally would be in like a walk zone, and they are terrified to send their children, so we’ve stood up additional transportation there. We’ve stood up food support. Our social work team and others have been amazing. We also, for staff that are scared to leave their home, we stood up ways for them to have groceries delivered and so forth. So, the impact is pretty significant on our district,” said Brenda Lewis, Fridley superintendent. 

On Tuesday, the anniversary of President Trump’s inauguration, students in classrooms in Minneapolis — and also nationwide — walked out in protest of ICE, the latest in the weeks of demonstrations against the federal enforcement efforts, which have come under mounting criticism.

Minneapolis Police Chief Brian O’Hara has said he is fearful of the “moment where it all explodes,” and Minnesota Sen. Amy Klobuchar (D) said she recently met with school officials and heard “horror stories” of ICE encounters with kids and parents. 

“Little kids scared. Dangerous encounters. This is no longer about a fraud investigation. If it was 3000 ICE agents wouldn’t be hanging outside of schools & wrestling people to the ground. They wouldn’t be chasing door dash delivery workers into homes. As they torment our state with threats, never give up on democracy and our resilience. Don’t take the bait. Be peaceful and stay safe,” Klobuchar said in a statement, referring to the Trump administration’s fraud investigations into day care centers in the state. 

The Hill has reached to Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz (D) for comment. 

Since the Trump administration rescinded guidance keeping ICE away from school property last year, administrators have had to create game plans for if agents try to enter their grounds. 

And while there has been no confirmed incident of ICE entering a school building, one Minneapolis high school saw ICE agents come onto school property in what the Department of Homeland Security said was in pursuit of a suspect.  

When ICE officers were near Roosevelt High School, DHS said a crowd surrounded them and became violent, leading the agents to use pepper spray and other crowd control tactics. Bystanders said the agents were the aggressors and started shoving people.  

“At no point was a school, students, or staff targeted, and agents would not have been near this location if not for the dangerous actions of this individual,” a DHS spokesperson said. 

Carlos Mariani Rosa, who sits on the board of a Spanish immersion school in St. Paul, said attendance has dropped from a typical 90-plus percent to around 60 percent in recent weeks.  

“Some of our teachers are visiting the homes to, one, provide some reassurance for people … these are teachers delivering food and household items,” said Rosa, who is also the executive director of Minnesota Education Equity Partnership.  

“In many schools across the state … we conducted webinars and trainings for groups of superintendents, teachers, even bus drivers and custodial staff, both charters and regular public schools, as they all pretty hungry about learning what can we do to keep our school as safe as possible,” he added. 

The Hill has reached out to the Education Department for comment. 

Worries are growing about what type of impact these disruptions will have on students, from learning loss to actual trauma.

“I heard from someone that teaches middle schools that the middle school hallways are quiet, that the students are quiet, and that’s a great concern, so educators are trying to find ways to engage their students, to keep that learning happening,” said Monica Byron, president of Education Minnesota.  

Teachers are “are seeing the impacts from our students disengaging and withdrawing, and when learning stuff, that’s a great concern for all,” she added.
